questions.q: Salim Ali is best known as a pioneer in which field of science?
questions.explanation: Salim Ali was India's foremost ornithologist, whose surveys and books transformed the study of Indian birds.

questions.q: By what popular nickname is Salim Ali widely remembered?
options: The Father of the Green Revolution
questions.explanation: Salim Ali is affectionately called 'The Birdman of India' for his lifelong work studying the country's birds.

questions.q: Salim Ali devoted his career to studying which group of animals?
questions.explanation: Salim Ali specialised in birds, conducting systematic surveys across the Indian subcontinent.

questions.q: Salim Ali was a citizen of which country?
questions.explanation: Salim Ali was an Indian ornithologist born in Bombay (Mumbai).

questions.q: Salim Ali's work is closely associated with which broader movement in India?
questions.explanation: His surveys and writings helped shape India's wildlife conservation and environmental movement.

questions.q: Salim Ali is famous for authoring popular field guides about Indian birds. What kind of books are these primarily?
options: Bird identification and natural history books
questions.explanation: He wrote widely used handbooks and field guides that made bird identification accessible to amateurs and scientists alike.

questions.q: Which natural history organisation in Bombay (Mumbai) was Salim Ali long associated with?
questions.explanation: Salim Ali was a leading figure of the Bombay Natural History Society (BNHS) for much of his career.

questions.q: What was the main method Salim Ali used to study birds across India?
options: Systematic field surveys of bird populations
questions.explanation: He carried out extensive systematic ornithological field surveys of regions across the subcontinent.

questions.q: Salim Ali helped popularise the appreciation of birds among which audience?
options: The general public and amateur birdwatchers
questions.explanation: His accessible writing brought birdwatching and natural history to a wide general audience in India.

questions.q: Salim Ali's surveys covered birds of which region most extensively?
questions.explanation: He surveyed birds across the Indian subcontinent, documenting their distribution and habits.

questions.q: In which year was Salim Ali born?
questions.explanation: Salim Moizuddin Abdul Ali was born on 12 November 1896 in Bombay.

questions.q: Salim Ali's classic introductory book is titled 'The Book of Indian ___'.
questions.explanation: 'The Book of Indian Birds', first published in 1941, became his best-known popular guide.

questions.q: Salim Ali co-authored the monumental multi-volume 'Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan' with which ornithologist?
questions.explanation: He wrote the ten-volume handbook together with the American ornithologist S. Dillon Ripley.

questions.q: A famous bird survey by Salim Ali as a young man focused on the breeding biology of which bird?
questions.explanation: His early study of the nesting and breeding of the Baya Weaver established his reputation as a field ornithologist.

questions.q: Salim Ali played a key role in saving which famous bird sanctuary, now a Ramsar site and national park in Rajasthan?
questions.explanation: He campaigned to protect the Keoladeo (Bharatpur) wetland, a renowned haven for birds in Rajasthan.

questions.q: Salim Ali was awarded one of India's highest civilian honours. Which one did he receive?
questions.explanation: He was honoured with the Padma Vibhushan, India's second-highest civilian award, in 1976.

questions.q: A bird research institute in Coimbatore is named after Salim Ali. It is abbreviated as:
questions.explanation: The Salim Ali Centre for Ornithology and Natural History (SACON) in Coimbatore is named in his honour.

questions.q: Salim Ali wrote a well-known autobiography. What is its title?
questions.explanation: His autobiography, 'The Fall of a Sparrow', recounts his life and ornithological career.

questions.q: In which decade did Salim Ali pass away?
questions.explanation: Salim Ali died on 20 June 1987, in the 1980s, at the age of 90.

questions.q: Salim Ali advocated strongly for which conservation approach in India?
options: Protecting natural habitats and bird sanctuaries
questions.explanation: He pushed for the protection of habitats and the creation and preservation of bird sanctuaries.

questions.q: Salim Ali's interest in birds was sparked in childhood by an encounter with which bird?
options: A yellow-throated sparrow he shot with an air gun
questions.explanation: As a boy he was intrigued by an unusual yellow-throated sparrow, an episode he credited with sparking his lifelong interest.

questions.q: Salim Ali studied ornithology in Germany under which influential ornithologist at the Berlin natural history museum?
questions.explanation: He trained briefly under Erwin Stresemann, a leading ornithologist, at the Zoological Museum in Berlin around 1929–30.

questions.q: Which series of regional bird surveys did Salim Ali conduct, often funded by Indian princely states, in the 1930s–40s?
options: Ornithological surveys of states such as Hyderabad, Travancore-Cochin and others
questions.explanation: He carried out sponsored regional bird surveys of several Indian states, building a detailed picture of the subcontinent's avifauna.

questions.q: Which long-lost Indian bird was famously rediscovered in 1986 by the Bombay Natural History Society, the institution Salim Ali led?
questions.explanation: Jerdon's Courser, unseen for decades, was rediscovered in Andhra Pradesh in 1986 by BNHS ornithologist Bharat Bhushan; the other options are extinct birds never found in India.

questions.q: Salim Ali received an honour from a British ornithological body. Which prestigious medal did he receive?
options: The Gold Medal of the British Ornithologists' Union
questions.explanation: He was awarded the Gold Medal of the British Ornithologists' Union (1967), a major international recognition of his work.

questions.q: Salim Ali also received an international conservation honour. Which one did he receive in 1976?
options: The J. Paul Getty Wildlife Conservation Prize
questions.explanation: He was awarded the J. Paul Getty Wildlife Conservation Prize in 1976 for his conservation contributions.

questions.q: What is the approximate scope of the 'Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan' co-written by Salim Ali and Ripley?
questions.explanation: The Handbook is a comprehensive ten-volume scholarly reference covering the birds of the region.

questions.q: In recognition of his contributions, Salim Ali was nominated to which body of the Indian Parliament in 1985?
options: The Lok Sabha by direct election
questions.explanation: He was nominated to the Rajya Sabha, India's upper house, in 1985 in recognition of his distinguished service to science and conservation.

questions.q: Salim Ali's family background and early business venture before he turned fully to birds involved which trade?
options: The family's tungsten and timber business in Burma
A diamond mine in South Africa
questions.explanation: As a young man he spent time managing the family's tungsten-mining and timber interests in Burma before committing to ornithology.

questions.q: Several species and a sanctuary commemorate Salim Ali. Which of these is named after him?
questions.explanation: Salim Ali's Fruit Bat (Latidens salimalii), among other taxa and a sanctuary, was named in his honour.
